title: iPhone 17 Pro Vs Samsung S25 Ultra ! 🔥
transcript: Friends, the time has come for Flagship Comparison of the Year. Today we will compare the iPhone 17 Pro vs Samsung Galaxy S25 Ultra.  Every year our subscribers get excited for this detailed comparison video and want to see if Samsung has finally managed to beat Apple or if Apple 's latest Pro series still comes out ahead.  Be it the display, performance or especially when it comes to the camera.  First of all let's talk about design and build quality. This Apple has finally changed the design language on its 17 Pro series and after a long wait of 5 years, we got to see this new refreshed design.   The durability of the S25 Ultra is quite impressive.  The titanium build is evident here, and it feels quite sturdy when you hold it in your hand. In terms of build quality, the S25 Ultra is better than the 17 Pro.  The colors and picture quality in the rest of the display are quite amazing. Both are flagship phones. One difference is that you will get support for HDR 10 Plus on the S25 Ultra.  And DOB Vision is also supported on the iPhone 17 Pro. Moving on to the performance part. Apple's A19 Pro chip is provided on the iPhone 17 Pro. We also get to see 12GB of RAM here.  The Galaxy S25 Ultra also comes with a flagship chipset.  Snapdragon 8L chipset has been provided and we also get 12GB RAM on top of it.  Scores around 24 lax are available on the iPhone 17 Pro.  At the same time, the Galaxy S25 Ultra touches the score of 3 million friends is still far ahead of the iPhone 17 Pro here.  The gaming experience turns out to be much better on the S25 Ultra because the display size is much larger here compared to the iPhone 17 Pro. But if you buy 17 Pro Max, you will get the same display size as S25 Ultra. We get to see vapor chamber cooling on both the phones. Because of this, if you play low graphics games like BGMI etc., the temperature does not go up much. Both phones worked well under 40°, as we tested.  But when it comes to graphics-intensive gaming performance, then friends, the iPhone 17 Pro works much better there.  Friends, let's move to the next step on the most important part which is going to be for camera comparison.  And on the iPhone we get to see a warmer tone in photos.  Whereas on S25 Ultra you get slightly saturated photos. And ready to upload on social media type photos.  It depends on you what kind of color profile you like.  You can make the remaining changes inside the color settings.  Side by side video comparison between the iPhone 17 Pro and the S25 Ultra.  4K is 60fps sampled. Outdoor condition.  It is a very noisy environment. How is the microphone quality?  We have shown it to you here. Rest you can also shift to ultrawide.  You can also see the video recording and how wide the iPhone is, how wide the Samsung is here. After ultrawide, let's move on to telephoto lenses. 3X optical zoom is now available on Samsung. And friends, we have 4X optical zoom on iPhone.  So here you can see what kind of optical quality is coming out on both the phones.

title: This iPhone 17 Pro Max Case is ALMOST Perfect (UAG Monarch Pro)
transcript: Apple's new iPhone 17 Pro models have a big problem. This year, the Pro phones use aluminum. It's lighter. It can run cooler, but it dents and scratches more easily. I've trusted thin cases for years. This time, that wasn't enough. One three-foot drop left real damage in [music] spots the case should have protected. So, I'm testing six rugged armor cases, starting with the UAG Monarch Pro. It might be the most expensive case I've ever bought in my history of iPhones. Is it really worth the insane $80 price tag? Let's find out. Now, out of the box, the Monarch Pro looks like a tool, not a fashion piece. It has UAG's armor style with a clean carbon fiber look. The lines are sharp and strong. In the hand, it feels sturdy, but not too heavy. It's also easy to put on and take off, which is rare for a rugged case. The camera ring sits higher than the lenses. Two small raised lips at the bottom match that height, [music] so when you set the phone down on its back, it doesn't rock. The edge texture is even all the way around. It never feels slippery when I'm scrolling or filming quick clips. Inside, UAG says it uses five layers. a rigid [music] polycarbonate plate, metal hardware, a shockabsorbing core, and an impact resistant rubber [music] surround. The case doesn't flex much, and the corners feel solid. I'm still not sure where the metal hardware is, but the frame feels very firm. Now, let's talk about how it handles during the day. At first, it felt a bit bulky. After some time with it, that changed. Compared to the Subcase Beetle Mag I used, this one actually feels lighter and more balanced. The contoured edges and anti-slip texture help a lot. I can use it one-handed for longer. The big buttons are easy to press and give a clear, clicky feel. Along the sides, the small honeycomb areas add grip without catching on my pockets. Next, let's check out Mags Safe. Mags Safe performance is solid with the case on my iPhone. And even testing the case by itself, the lock felt about the same as using a naked iPhone. The Subcase Beetle Mag I tested did feel stronger, but that setup is also heavier. Extra weight can help mounts hold steady, so the grip there may feel tighter. Moving on, let's look at the new camera button. UAG leaves the new camera button uncovered, and that choice [music] has its pros and cons. The pro is that there will be fewer accidental presses when you grip the phone. [music] The con is that you will have to press a little harder to activate the camera button because the case walls sit higher around the button. Half press and full press still work, but you need more pressure. For me, that trade-off is worth it. I'd rather avoid random button presses. Now, let's see how this case handles multiple drops. On paper, the Monarch Pro is a tank. UJ claims 25 ft drop protection and says it can survive 26 drops from 4 ft with no phone damage. Let's see how true this second claim is. Now, after what happened to my iPhone 17 Pro Max and Apple's clear case, I didn't want to risk more expensive repairs, [music] so I used a realistic dummy 17 Pro Max instead. The dummy has glass on the front and back, a plastic frame, and similar size and weight. It's not identical, but it's useful to see how a case absorbs impact. I logged 18 total drops with the Monarch Pro on the dummy phone. Before starting, I photographed the dumy's existing scuffs so I could track any new damage. After drops three and four, the dumy's front screen started separating from the body. I'll explain why that likely happened later in the video. After drop six, [music] the screen came completely loose, but there was still no crack on the glass and no body dents. I saw that there was a weight glued to the back of the glass screen, which likely made the dummy foam more front heavy and explains why the glass screen was wanting to separate from the body during the drops. I taped the screen back on to keep testing. By drop 11, the tape was tearing and the screen looked ready to separate again. The case, however, still felt tight around the corners and edges. At drop 18, I finally saw new damage on another corner of the dummy phone. I stopped there to save the dummy for future tests and review the results. After removing all the tape, I found no cracks on the screen, no new damage on the body and no marks around the camera area on the dummy. On the case itself, I noticed a tiny worn spot near the camera button. Everything else looked clean with [music] no splits, chips, or deep scuffs. If I had started with a brand new dummy phone, I think it might have gotten closer to that 26 drops number. Even so, the Monarch Pro performed well. Based on these drops, I'm confident this case would protect my real iPhone better than a thin clear case, especially around the corners and camera area. Now, there is a major drawback to this case. There is no kickstand. At this price, I expected at least a simple landscape stand for videos or cooking. UAG sells another model with a stand, so it's odd that this one doesn't have it. There is also a separate UAG magnetic ring stand accessory you can buy to stick onto this case, but it really should be integrated together. So, [music] is the UAG Monarch Pro case worth the insane AOL price tag? If you want tough protection for an aluminum iPhone, this case does a lot right. It feels solid, goes on and off easily, and the grip and buttons are great. The raised lip keeps the camera and screen safe on flat surfaces. Mag Safe [music] performance is solid and close to a naked iPhone. The Subcase Beetle Mag felt stronger, but that setup is heavier, which can make mounts feel more stable. In my 18 drop test with the dummy phone, the case held up well. The dummy screen separated, but the glass didn't crack, [music] and the body and camera area stayed clean. The big miss is the kickstand. At this price, I expected one built-in. If hands-free viewing matters, that could be a dealbreaker for you. My take is choose the Monarch [music] Pro if you value protection, grip, and day-to-day comfort over extras. If you need a stand or the strongest magnets, look elsewhere. I'll keep testing this case on my real iPhone. If it keeps the edges and corners clean over time, that alone could make the price worth it this year. Now, if you want to see my review of one of the cheapest, [music] rugged iPhone 17 Pro Max cases you can buy, check out this video right here. Thanks for watching, and I'll see you in the next one.

title: iPhone 17 Pro, Air e 17. Qual è il MIGLIORE da PRENDERE ?
transcript: Today I'm putting the new iPhone 17, iPhone Air, and iPhone 17 Pro through their paces with one goal in mind: to recommend the best iPhone to buy this year.  To do this, I spent the last few weeks with them to understand which compromises are worth accepting and which are not.  So stay with me because at the end of the video I will choose the overall winner and I assure you the result may not be what you expect.  Before we get into it, I'd like to ask you a small favor: like the video and subscribe to my channel.  Two completely free gestures for you, but which will be of great help in my growth here on YouTube.  Thank you.  What's this year?  Let's go and find out. First, I'll give you a quick description of each model in no particular order, listing the pros and cons, and then give you a final verdict at the end of the video .  I'll start with the phone that has caused the most discussion, the iPhone Air.  With this model, Apple had only one goal: to push the design beyond all limits.  In fact, its first Pro is precisely its design, it is thin, only 5.6 6 mm and it is also light, only 165 g. Forget all the other phones you've ever used because the feel in your pocket is unmatched.  If your priority is a phone that disappears in your hand, in your pocket or bag, the Air is definitely the one for you.  The second pro is the wow effect that it made me feel, as soon as I picked it up for the first time, a sensation that I have only experienced twice before.  The first in 2010 with iPhone 4 and then in 2017 with iPhone X. Sensations of pure quality, robustness and beauty.  It is truly the most premium iPhone of this year with its titanium frame that makes it perfect to hold, putting even the new 17 Pro and 17 Pro Max to shame. Third Pro is the display.  Curiously, the ER is a 6.5-inch display that represents an excellent middle ground for those who want a complete visual experience without the weight and bulk of the Pro Max, being able to count on the same peak brightness on adaptive refresh rate technology up to 120 Hz, combined with the always-on Display. Visually it is uncompromising. But now let's get to the cons.  The first is battery life because to achieve this thinness in design the battery was the first victim. Its battery life is slightly lower than that of the iPhone 17, not to mention the 17 Pro Max which is almost impressive this year.  It is no coincidence that Apple has launched a MaxSafe battery, compatible only with the iPhone Air, which serves to extend its battery life, but which nullifies the wow effect of the design.  Secondly, there is a negative aspect to the camera system, as there is only one rear camera like on the iPhone 16. It is a fantastic 48 megapixel fusion camera, but you will not have either the ultra- wide angle for landscapes or the telephoto lens with optical zoom for creative photos.  There are also many shooting and video recording modes missing.  There are no macros, there is no space mode and there is not even the option to record in cinema mode.  In short, big sacrifices.  for those who love photography.  The third con is mostly a sum of things that make this air appear a bit below par. Charging via caves, the MaxSafe one. No stereo sound, no spatial sound, and audio quality you would never expect from an Apple device.  In summary, I can therefore conclude that the iPhone Air is a highly emotional product, a concept phone transformed into a consumer product , which was perhaps a design exercise that previews the rumored iPhone Fold that is coming soon.   It’s drop- dead gorgeous, but the compromises are heavy at €1,239, which is €260 more than the iPhone 17. Then there are the iPhone 17 Pro models. If you’re a video content creator or simply can’t accept compromises, this is your iPhone.  Primo Pro is the triple camera system.  Here we have the complete package, main, ultra-wide and telephoto all 48 megapixels. In addition, the capture functions, such as photos in Pro RAW, video encoding which now reaches up to Pro Raw with the gem of 4K slow motion.  at 120 frames per second makes it unrivaled in this regard .  According to Pro, the prolonged performance over time that now, thanks to the cooling system that includes for the first time in an iPhone, the vapor chamber will guarantee us to keep the temperature under control and not give up the performance of the A19 Pro sock or the brightness of the display that lowers after much longer.  Ideal for those who film a lot or those who game like crazy.  Third Pro is the autonomy.  excellent on both models, but especially on the 17 Pro Max it has reached impressive levels, making it the best ever.  The day is more than guaranteed and on many days I arrived in bed with over 40% battery remaining, unreachable.  But even the 17 Pro and 17 Pro Max have flaws. This year the first is called aluminum, the material chosen for the top of the range, which gives a feeling of quality and robustness that is decidedly inferior to the stainless steel used up to the 14 Pro and especially the titanium used on the 15 and 16 Pro and which we also find this year on the Air. Apple certainly opted for aluminum, for its high capacity to manage heat better, and that's fine.  However, as we have seen in many cases that have emerged on the web, the robustness and resistance of this material has been called into question, which on a Pro is perhaps a bit too tight.  The second downside is that they are bulky phones, especially the Pro Max. If you use the phone one-handed or want to carry it in your pocket without feeling it, the Pros are a step down in terms of comfort, both due to their large size and weight and the standard platform that protrudes too much and risks scratches or dents.  The 17 Pro are therefore powerful work tools. If your work depends on the camera, get it without hesitation, otherwise wait a moment longer because the starting prices of €1339 for the 17 Pro and €1489 for the Pro Max are decidedly significant, considering that some of the more pro features are actually only useful to a narrow niche of audio and video professionals.  And here we come to the standard model, the iPhone 17, the one that is easiest to underestimate, but let me tell you that for the vast majority of people this is the right iPhone?  The first winning feature is the fact that it hides an iPhone Pro. This year it features a display with promotion technology like the Pros, with the always-on display once again like the Pros and it has the exact same 6.3- inch screen as the 17 Pro. And finally, the same starting storage space increased from 128 to 256 GB.  As you may have understood, the huge technical gap that existed until last year has been filled.  The second advantage of the iPhone 17 is the photo and video sector, because we find both 48 megapixel Fusion photographic sensors , which brings it even closer to the top of the range model and it also has the same identical center stage front camera equipped on all the other models. Third and final advantage, given the first two, is the quality-price ratio.  Its €979 makes it very interesting because with a saving of almost €400 from the 17 Pro model it is a complete iPhone that now has very little to envy of the top of the range.  On the other hand, I struggle to identify any for this model without resorting to subjective arguments that may, however, be important to many of you. Some need the 17 Pro Max’s huge display, others the telephoto lens, and still others the insane battery life.   there is.  But to have all these things you need a 17 Pro Max with an additional cost of over 500 and which in my opinion this year does not completely justify the difference as it has been in the past. So, what's the best iPhone to buy?  As you may have gathered, my verdict is clear: the standard iPhone 17 is the best iPhone for the average user, and even for most advanced users.  offers the perfect combination of reliability, power and price. Battery life is superior to the R and is enough to compete with the 17 Pro. The camera is excellent for 95% of situations and the cost savings are significant and probably in a few weeks, as Black Friday or the holidays approach, the first online discounts will also start to arrive .  You will no longer have to pay hundreds of euros more for pro features that you will never use. Because if you're looking for a phone that lasts, takes great photos, and won't leave you plugged in for a charger mid-afternoon, the iPhone 17 is the rational choice, the smart choice, and, in my opinion, the best choice.  What I'm most interested in now is hearing from you what you think and whether you agree with my ranking.  So I'll wait for you below in the comments.  That 's really all for today's video.  We'll see you very soon here on the channel with lots of new videos.  A big hello from Francesco. [music]

title: iPhone 17 Pro Disassembly & Reassembly | Housing Swap & Repair
transcript: Hello everyone, and welcome to HowFixit. In this  video, I’ll show you how to completely disassemble   the iPhone 17 Pro so you can replace any faulty  components. You’ll learn how to change the   housing, screen, battery, logic board, cameras,  speakers, Face ID, power and volume buttons,   and charging port. This guide also covers the  full reassembly process so you can successfully   complete the repair. Tools You’ll Need  Before getting started, here’s  what you’ll need for this repair:  Precision screwdriver set (Pentalobe 0.8  mm, T4 Torx, Tri-Wing 0.8 mm, Phillips 1 mm,   Standoff 1.5 mm) Plastic pry tools  Tweezers Suction Cup  Screen disassembly tool – essential for  safely separating the display from the frame  9-volt battery and two wires  with clamps (for battery removal)  Adhesive sealing tape (to  restore water resistance)  I’ve included links to all the tools and  replacement parts in the video description below.  Before we get started, here’s  A precision screwdriver set, plastic pry tools,  a pair of tweezers, a suction cup, and a screen   disassembly tool — essential for safely separating  the display from the frame. To remove the battery,   you’ll need a 9-volt battery with two wires  and clamps, and to restore water resistance,   you’ll need adhesive sealing tape. I’ve  included links to all the tools and   replacement parts in the video description below. For the best results, I highly recommend using   a magnetic mat to organize screws and  small components during disassembly.   The iPhone 17 Pro contains dozens of screws  of varying sizes, and a magnetic mat helps   keep everything secure and properly organized  for reassembly. Alright, let’s get started.  First, power off your iPhone and remove the  two screws located next to the USB-C port.  Next, heat the screen. Warming the  lower edge softens the waterproof   adhesive — apply gentle, even heat for  60 to 90 seconds at about 90 °C (194 °F).  Place a suction cup near the bottom edge of  the screen, then insert a thin blade into the   gap between the chassis and the display to  loosen the adhesive. Once a small gap forms,   switch to a plastic pry tool. Keep  the surface warm as you work your   way around the edges — a well-heated  screen separates much more easily.  The screen is tightly glued to the frame,  so apply steady, even pressure as you go.  Ensure the surface stays warm  as you work around the edges;   this helps make adhesive removal easier.  At the top edge, two hinges connect the  display to the housing; once you reach them,   push slightly downward to release. Lift the display like a book from the   side. Remove the screw securing the bracket  over the display and front-sensor connectors,   then carefully disconnect the cables. Next, remove the 14 screws securing the   large metal plate that covers the  battery, and lift the plate off.  The battery is attached to the back of this  cooling plate and doesn’t need to be removed   unless you’re replacing it. If you are replacing  it, you’ll need the 9-volt battery and clamps.  Peel off the small tab at the bottom of the  battery. Connect the black wire to the negative   terminal and the red wire to the positive terminal  of the 9-volt battery. Attach the red clamp to the   silver tab and the black clamp to the uncoated  light area of the plate. Hold the connection for   about 90 seconds to soften the adhesive. If the  clamps come loose, simply repeat the process.   Once the adhesive releases,  lift the battery out gently.  If your replacement battery includes pre-installed  adhesive, remove the liner and place it onto the   plate. If not, install new adhesive strips  manually. I’ve included a link to replacement   battery adhesive below the video. Now let’s move back to the phone.   Unscrew the bracket securing the Face ID  cable and disconnect it from the logic   board. Remove the entire Face ID module. Next, remove the three screws holding the   camera module. Disconnect its flex cables from  the logic board and lift the camera module out.  Unscrew and remove the earpiece speaker.  Disconnect all remaining cables attached to  the logic board. Gently lift the board from   the housing, then remove the screw  securing the small rear bracket.   Disconnect the wireless-charging-coil  cable and set the logic board aside.  Now remove the ribbon cable that includes  the flash diode and rear microphone.  Be mindful of the small antenna  brackets — keep them with their   corresponding screws to ensure  proper placement during reassembly.  Before detaching the cable,   lightly heat the surface to soften the  adhesive. Then lift the module carefully.   Replacing this cable assembly will fix  issues with the flash or rear microphone.  Remove the two screws securing the next bracket,   then warm the surface again before  taking out the LiDAR sensor module.  Remove the screws securing  the antenna to the chassis.   This antenna is glued to the chassis,   so gently heat the area first to  avoid damage when prying it up.  Remove the screws securing the Taptic Engine,   flip it over, disconnect the  ribbon cable, and lift it out.  Next, remove the screws holding the bottom  loudspeaker, then lift it from the chassis.  Unscrew the fasteners securing the USB-C  port and lower-microphone flex cable.  Because the charging-port flex is glued in,   heat the surface slightly before removal.  Once warm, peel it away carefully.  If your iPhone has charging or connection  issues, replacing this part will usually   fix the problem. Links to replacement  charging ports are in the description below.  To remove the volume buttons, unscrew the  screws holding the flex cable to the chassis,   then gently detach and remove it. If  your volume buttons aren’t working,   replacing this cable should restore them.  Next, unscrew and remove the Power  Button and Control Button flex cable.   Both buttons share the same flex assembly. If either button fails, replacing this cable   will fix the issue. However, note that the  Control Button combines both a sensor and a   mechanical component. The sensor is welded to  the chassis, making it extremely difficult to   replace without changing the entire housing.  In addition, the touch-sensitive part of the   Control Button is paired to your Apple  ID. When replacing the housing assembly,   calibration using Repair Assistant will be  required — I’ll cover this later during testing.  The wireless-charging coil is attached to the  back-glass cover. It can be replaced separately   or together with the glass. The back glass itself  can be removed the same way as the screen — by   heating and gently prying around the edges. At this point, all modules and parts have been   removed. The iPhone 17 Pro is now completely  disassembled. If your phone’s frame is bent or   damaged, this is the perfect time to replace it or  install new parts. All replacement links for the   iPhone 17 Pro are in the video description below. Before beginning reassembly, take a short   break — this helps maintain focus  and precision during the final steps. Reassembly and Important Notes During reassembly, make sure   every screw goes back in the right place. Using the wrong screw can cause serious   issues — like gaps or flexing  in the frame, stripped threads,   or even electrical shorts if it’s too long. Always keep your screws organized and match   them to their exact positions  before tightening anything.  Keep connectors clean and dust-free. Dust  or debris can cause poor connections,   unstable performance, or even short circuits. Use  only an anti-static brush to clean connectors — it   safely removes dust without generating  static electricity that could damage chips.  Double-check each cable connection. A  loose or misaligned connector can cause   unresponsive buttons, cameras, or sensors,  or even overheating. Taking an extra moment   to ensure each one is properly seated can  save you from reopening the phone later.  Common post-repair issues — such as malfunctioning  parts — usually result from loose connections,   dust in connectors, or incorrect screw placement.   Careful attention during reassembly  will help you avoid all of these.  Place the cooling plate with the battery back  into the phone and secure it with a few screws.  Reconnect the display, power on the  iPhone, and verify that it boots.  After replacing any major components, use  Repair Assistant to calibrate the device.  Go to Settings, then select  General, and then the About section.  You will see a new section — Parts & Service  History. Go to this section and follow the   on-screen prompts to start calibration.  Your iPhone must be connected to Wi-Fi   and have at least 20 percent battery to  complete the process. After calibration,   the phone will restart automatically. Then  verify that every module — Face ID, cameras,   buttons, charging port, microphones,  sensors, and speakers — works correctly.  Once testing is complete,  let’s restore water resistance.  Remove the old adhesive tape from the  iPhone’s frame. Once the iPhone is opened,   the adhesive tape loses its sealing properties  and can no longer protect your device from   water and dust. To restore water resistance,  you’ll need to apply new waterproof sealing   tape around the frame. However, if you  don’t have the new adhesive tape on hand,   it’s best to leave the old tape in  place until you’re ready to replace it.  Use a cotton swab dipped in isopropyl alcohol   (91% or higher) to clean any  remaining residue from the frame.  Place the cooling plate with the battery into  the iPhone and secure it with fourteen screws.  Carefully align the new adhesive tape with the  edges of the frame, ensuring proper placement.  Press down along the perimeter using  a plastic spudger to secure it firmly.  Once the tape is applied, peel off  the top liner to expose the adhesive.  Reconnect the display and sensor connectors,  replace the bracket, and tighten the screw.  Press gently along the screen edges to ensure a  snug seal, then reinstall the two screws near the   charging port to finish assembly. If you found this guide helpful,   give us a thumbs up and subscribe for  more tech tips and guides. Visit our   website for more insights and Do-It-Yourself  guides. Remember — every tech challenge is an   opportunity to learn and grow. Thanks for watching  HowFixit, and we’ll see you in the next one!

title: iPhone 17 Pro Max vs. Pixel 10 Pro XL vs. Galaxy S25 Ultra: Ultimativer Vergleich
transcript: These are the best smartphones from the major manufacturers.  The iPhone 17 Pro Max, Google Pixel 10 Pro XL and of course the Samsung Galaxy S25 Ultra. We compared the three very closely , used each one as our main device one after the other, examined their performance, and were particularly thorough in our camera comparison.  Quite surprisingly for me, the Pixel beats the iPhone by a mile in two areas .  Be sure to watch the comparison to the end, because there are some surprises. [Music] Let's start with the hardware.  The iPhone 17 Pro Max has an A19 Pro chip and the Samsung Galaxy S25 Ultra has a Snapdragon 8 Elite for Galaxy.  The Pixel 10 Pro XL comes with a Tensor G5 processor, and all models are available with 256 GB to 1 TB of storage, although only the iPhone is also offered with 2 TB. Samsung and Apple install 12 GB of RAM, and Google 16 GB.  In reality, all three feel very powerful and always fast. No matter what you do, you almost never have to wait for anything. However, there are significant differences in benchmarks and gaming.  In Geekbench 6, the Pixel's single-core performance is n't bad at all, but in all other aspects it lags far behind the competition.  In the multicore test, the Samsung is slightly better than the Apple, but the iPhone beats them all in the single-core and graphics tests. Our 3DMK comparison, which tests game performance, is also interesting.  Here, the S25 Ultra is actually better than the iPhone, and the Pixel once again takes last place. Apple wins big in the stress test.  Here it achieves a stability of 69.6%, while the Pixel reaches 58.8% and the S25 Ultra only 43.1%. The Pro Max can therefore maintain its performance consistently for longer periods, which speaks to excellent cooling.  The other two need to downclock faster, otherwise the chip would overheat.  In reality, this means that you can play games on your iPhone for longer at the highest graphics settings and that video editing exports are faster, simply because the chip doesn't have to downclock as much .  Here in our studio, we're all Fortnite fans and of course we tested the game on all three smartphones.  Unfortunately, the Pixel reflects its benchmark results and performs worst here. You can play Fortnite, but only at 60 FPS with epic settings, and you can't select anything higher. With the S25 Ultra, you can select 120 FPS and it actually runs surprisingly stable at over 100 FPS even during longer sessions.  The graphics settings in Fortnite cannot be transferred one-to-one from platform to platform .  On the iPhone 17 Pro Max, 120 FPS can only be selected as an option if you set medium graphics settings. It then runs perfectly at between 100 and 120 FPS and actually looks slightly better than on Samsung with epic settings, but really only slightly.  However, if you set the iPhone to epic, you can only select 60 FPS, but then the graphics look a bit better than on the Samsung and run quite smoothly at 55 to 60 FPS.   If you're looking for a gaming phone, the iPhone 17 Pro Max is the clear winner.  Games run perfectly with the highest graphics settings, even over extended periods.  Generally, the iPhone is better if you need high performance.  In Number Fusion and Instagram Edits, it renders videos significantly faster and is also more stable than a Snapdragon 8 Elite. But of course, if you prefer Android, the S25 Ultra is better than the Pixel in this respect .  I was quite surprised by my camera comparison.  In JPEG or ALSO format, the 200-megapixel main camera of the S25 Ultra captures slightly more detail than the 48-megapixel camera of the iPhone 17 Pro Max. Even more surprisingly, the Pixel 10 Pro XL, with its 50 megapixels, is sharper than both and wins by a landslide.  Even in RAW format, the Pixel has more detail than the iPhone, and the S25 Ultra is truly impressive.  However, the Google phone has the worst ultra-wide-angle camera of the three, and here the Samsung is minimally, but really only very minimally, sharper than the iPhone. However, the iPhone delivers better results in RAW format.  I suspect this is also due to the fact that Apple even processes and sharpens RAW files somewhat. The iPhone truly lives up to its reputation as a top-class camera when it comes to telephoto cameras .  Although Google and Samsung offer a 5x zoom, the iPhone, with its 4x zoom, is slightly sharper.  Furthermore, in reality, 4x is much more practical than 5x.  Especially when photographing portraits, a 4X camera is more useful than a 5X camera.  Well, the boss and details aren't everything.  For example, the iPhone takes the best photos in portrait mode.  Unfortunately, Samsung's cameras can only be 12 megapixels in size, and the Pixel performed worst in our test when it came to cropping.  I also think that Apple is generally often better at automatic post-processing.  During a trip to Budapest, I tested the Pixel camera more thoroughly.  Simply because I was so impressed by the quality, and it really is.  The long exposure function is really practical and truly useful.  Unfortunately, I 've also noticed that you can only take 350 megapixel RAW photos in quick succession.  Then you need to take a few seconds' break. Something is buffering in the background. That's a real shame.  For me, that's definitely a major drawback.  The other two don't have this problem.  At night, the 17 Pro Max takes significantly better photos than the S25 Ultra.  And the Pixel is on par with the iPhone and sometimes even shows a little more detail.  Let's move on to the front cameras. Here, the Pixel 10 Pro XL has slightly more detail than the iPhone, even though its new 18-megapixel camera is high-resolution.  And while the Ultra also offers a lot of detail, it produces significantly more noise in the shadows than the other two.  What's really cool about the iPhone is that, thanks to the new Center Stage camera, you can rotate photos and videos with the front camera both vertically and horizontally without having to rotate the phone .  If the pure, unedited photo quality is most important to you, then the Pixel is the clear winner.  I think it's really great in this respect .  However, when I consider all the points , I think the iPhone is the better choice for most people if you want a top-notch camera .  Photos look great, and I generally find unedited iPhone photos better than those from Samsung.  If you don't feel like putting too much effort into photography, most people will be able to consistently take better photos with an iPhone .  Now comes the big "but": unfortunately, you cannot manually control the iPhone camera.  Even with third-party apps like Halide, you can only set a maximum exposure time of one second, for example .  If you want to do long exposure photography, this is only possible if you use an app to combine many photos into one.  However, with the Samsung and Google cameras you can take beautiful, true manual long exposure photos .  If you want to use your smartphone more like a camera, I recommend the Pixel or the S25 Ultra.  The Pix also has a great long exposure mode that works even during the day. By the way, you may have noticed in the photo comparisons that we zoom in to at least 100% and often even to 200 and 300%. You only see significant differences then. Our comparison is therefore criticism at a very high level, and if you mainly publish photos on social media and are not a camera or photo nerd like me, all these differences are rather negligible. I find it quite sad that the Pixel 10 Pro XL performed rather poorly in our video test . The image itself looks good, but there is a constant slight stuttering. This is due to the image stabilization, which apparently isn't working properly.  I also noticed that it sharpens the image extremely at 2x zoom, which makes the video almost unusable.  Videos from the S25 Ultra look great, especially on a mobile phone.  However, when viewed on a large monitor, one notices an after-sharpening effect that is not quite as nice.  It's okay, but the iPhone 17 Pro Max looks more natural in comparison The iPhone is by far the best device for video recording.  The video shows many details without overly aggressive sharpening, and anyone who wants to can even try it out. I recorded and edited my entire Xiaomi Redmi Pad 2 Pro handson video with my iPhone without any professional accessories, including a microphone. If you watch the video, you'll see what you can expect from the iPhone without professional tools. Even more would be possible with professional tools .  These days, of course, every smartphone takes pretty good photos. But your photography is far more important than the hardware If you know how to use your phone's camera correctly, how to deal with light and perspective, and are familiar with the rules of composition, then you can take photos with any phone that look like they were taken by a professional photographer with a huge camera.  This is exactly the knowledge I teach you in my smartphone photography for beginners course .  If you want to take better photos , take a look at this. You can find the link in the description.  There are clear differences in design between the three .  The iPhone 17 Pro Max has an aluminum casing with a Seramic Shield back and front and looks to me like a phone for geeks and gamers. Especially the orange version, of course.  The S25 Ultra has a titanium frame and Gorilla Glass on the front and back, which gives it a rather industrial look to me. Google opted for an aluminum frame, also with Gorilla Glass, and to me it looks more like a designer phone.  Less for nerds.  All are IP68-rated for water and dust resistance, and at 8.2 mm the Samsung is the thinnest, followed by the 8.5 mm Pixel and 8.8 mm iPhone.  They all have a USB-C 3.2 port and can all be connected to an external monitor, but the iPhone lacks a desktop mode.   The latter will of course be done later via Face ID, while Samsung and Google offer a fingerprint reader.  The Pixel also has secure facial recognition, which can be used to unlock banking apps.  That's not possible with the Samsung.  There are some interesting differences in the displays. If the S25 Ultra and 17 Pro Max are exactly 6.9 inches in size, then the Pixel is slightly smaller at 6.8 inches.  They all have a pixel density of well over 450 PPI and therefore look very sharp.  And yes, they all support up to 120 Hz and they are all OLEDs.  With a peak brightness of 2600 nits, the S25 Ultra is dimmer than the Google and Apple phones, which each offer up to 3000 nits.  The standard brightness, i.e., when you manually set it to maximum, is significantly brighter on the Pixel than on the other two, and Apple is then only minimally brighter than the S25 Ultra. So if you want the brightest display in every situation , the Pixel is the clear winner.  If you want to write on your smartphone with a stylus , the S25 Ultra is the only choice.  The Sen works perfectly, is included, and Samsung Notes is a great notes app.  The other two can only be written on with capacitive pens, and these are very poor in comparison.  The speakers are excellent and pretty much equally good on all three.   It is almost impossible to tell the difference .  There are, of course, huge differences in the software. Both the Pixel and Galaxy run Android 16, with both manufacturers customizing the user interface.  Both will receive updates for 7 years.  The iPhone was released with iOS 26 and will also receive updates for a very long time , although Apple doesn't specify exactly how long, but certainly 7 years .  There are of course some differences in the software, although I think they aren't that significant anymore .  At least the iPhone now has a files app and you can connect external SSDs and so on .  Many apps are basically the same anyway.  Apple obviously has a really great ecosystem, and if you've always had an iPhone, a Mac, and an iPad, it hardly makes sense to switch to an Android device .  I think no one will doubt that Google offers by far the best AI features.  Samsung also has Gemini, sure, but Google AI can make the 100x zoom of the camera app usable.  This is hardly a photo anymore, but rather partly AI-generated, but really impressive, and the live translation function during phone calls works surprisingly well.  In our battery test, the iPhone leads slightly with 26.5 hours, followed by the S25 Ultra with 26.25 hours. The Pixel performs worst with 18.9 hours.  For this test, I let my HD YouTube video run in an endless loop at maximum brightness .  And yes, our test isn't perfect, because the Google phone is significantly brighter.  In fact, the runtimes are real and quite similar at the same brightness.  So, which of the three sets has the better smartphone?  The iPhone 17 Pro Max is better if you need a lot of power for gaming and even want to edit videos with a mobile phone.  It is also particularly good for video recordings as a camera phone, and especially for Apple fans.  The Pixel 10 Pro XL, on the other hand, is the better choice if you want the brightest possible display and the best AI in every situation. However, it is also an excellent choice if you want to take particularly sharp and detailed photos and are willing to accept compromises elsewhere, especially in video. Go for the Samsung Kreif if you need the S-Pern and want to take good videos and good photos , i.e., both, in unedited Mira and manually, as for long exposures.  It's also better than the Pixel if you don't want an iPhone but do want a gaming smartphone .  All these devices are quite expensive.  With the non- pro models, you get very similar functionality for many hundreds of euros less .  Be sure to check out our comparison of the iPhone 17 with the Pixel 10, because then you'll know if it's really worth spending so much more money .  M.

title: BENKS iPhone 17 Pro ArmorPro, Montage Case & GlassWarrior Protector Review
transcript: So, it's been 2 weeks with the iPhone 17 Pro. You may have seen my previous video where I covered the iPhone Air. That had to go back after two weeks of just not really being fit for my regular use with these phones. So, I've upgraded to the Pro. The Pro made the better choice for me because although the base model would have been a little bit better overall and cheaper, it just made sense to just go an extra £100 and just go all out. I'm not really a big fan of the two-tone. I really do wish they changed that next year. And I hear that they might be using a more all-in-one glass backing instead as well as their unibody. But it is definitely a heavier phone. The battery life has just been awesome. I've had zero complaints about the battery. I've not even had any battery warnings that much. Really enjoying the 3.0 USB speeds of the transfer. 2 GB in just mere seconds. Really, really good. The lenses have been awesome as well.
